subject: Surgery
question: Which of the following is true about the Tillaux sign?

options.a: Mass is felt in the periumbilical region
options.c: Mass is mobile perpendicular to mesentery attachment line

explanation:
Tillaux's sign is said to be positive if the mass/swelling is mobile perpendicular to the mesentery
attachment line. Tillaux’s sign is seen in a mesenteric cyst.
Mesenteric cysts occur either in the small intestine (60%) or the colon (40%) and can be classified
as the following:
• Chylolymphatic (most common type)
• Enterogenous
• Urogenital remnant
• Dermoid
• Other causes: traumatic hematoma and cyst formation, tuberculous cold abscess of mesentery
and hydatid cyst of the mesentery
They most commonly occur in adults with a mean age of 45 years. It is more common in females
than males (2:1). The most common presentation is a painless abdominal swelling near the
umbilicus, which is smooth and not moving with respiration with Tillaux's triad:
• Soft, smooth swelling in the umbilical region
• Freely mobile in a direction perpendicular to the mesentery (Tillaux's sign)
• Zone of resonance around the cyst
Other presentations include recurrent attacks of abdominal pain due to temporary impaction of a
food bolus in a segment of the bowel narrowed by the cyst and vomiting.
Complications of mesenteric cyst include torsion, rupture, infection and hemorrhage into the
cyst.
Ultrasonography and CT are used to demonstrate mesenteric cysts.
4773
Treatment:
• Chlyolymphatic cyst is managed by enucleation.
• Enterogenous cyst is managed by removal of the cyst with resection of the adjacent bowel as it
arises as a diverticulum or duplication from the adjacent bowel.
In cases where complete excision is not possible due to the close proximity to vital structures,
partial excision or marsupialization should be performed.

[Surgery-AIIMS-2020 (May and Nov INI-CET)-35-exp-image]

subject: Surgery
question: Match the correct answer related to the nerve injuries in various surgeries:
explanation:
A-1, B-2, C-3, and D-4 is the correct option.
A: During breast surgery, removal of the axillary lymph nodes is commonly done. This carries a
risk of injury to the long thoracic nerve, which originates from the C5-C7 roots of the brachial
plexus. It innervates the serratus anterior muscle. Injury to this nerve during surgery results in
paralysis of the serratus anterior muscle leading to winging of the scapula.
B: The external laryngeal nerve is a motor nerve that supplies the cricothyroid muscle. The close
relationship of the external laryngeal nerve to the superior thyroid artery puts the nerve at
potential risk when the artery is clamped during thyroid surgeries.
C: Excision of the submandibular gland results in potential risk to 3 cranial nerves:
• Marginal mandibular branch of the facial nerve
• Lingual nerve
• Hypoglossal nerve
D: The vagus nerve is at greater risk of injury during surgeries like thymectomy.

subject: Surgery
question:
A child is diagnosed with congenital hypertrophic pyloric stenosis, which among the following
can be seen in this child?
explanation:
4774
Patients with congenital hypertrophic pyloric stenosis (CHPS) will present with non-bilious
projectile vomiting and visible peristalsis observed moving from left to right (not from right to
left). Metabolic abnormalities include hypokalemia, hypochloremia and metabolic alkalosis.
CHPS occurs due to hypertrophy of the circular muscle of the pylorus, which results in
constriction and obstruction of the gastric outlet.
• Patients commonly present between 2 and 8 weeks of age.
• First-born male infants are at the highest risk.
• Infants usually present with projectile, non-bilious vomiting.
• Clinically, an olive-shaped mass is palpable in the right upper quadrant or epigastrium. When
the infant is given a test feed, visible peristalsis is usually observed moving from left to right (not
from right to left), followed by projectile vomiting.
Metabolic abnormality:
• Infants with hypertrophic pyloric stenosis develop a characteristic metabolic abnormality i.e.
hypokalemic hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis with paradoxical aciduria.
• Persistent vomiting results in loss of hydrochloric acid, leading to metabolic alkalosis (due to
loss of H+) and hypochloremia (due to loss of Cl-).
• Kidneys try to compensate for alkalosis by secreting Na+ and HCO3-. However, sodium loss
stimulates aldosterone secretion through the RAAS pathway.
• Aldosterone causes active reabsorption of sodium in exchange for K+ and H+ ions leading to the
development of hypokalemia and paradoxical aciduria, since the body initially loses H+ ions due
to the loss of HCl, and instead of compensating for that loss, the body is losing more H+ ions
through urine.
Management:
• The child should be rehydrated and the metabolic abnormality must be corrected before surgery.
• Ramstedt pyloromyotomy remains the standard surgical procedure for its treatment.

subject: Surgery
question: The components of qSOFA scoring include:
explanation:
The quick Sequential Organ Failure Assessment (qSOFA) scoring system does not rely on the
heart rate.
It is a bedside scoring system that may identify patients with suspected infection, who are at
greater risk for sepsis outside the intensive care unit (ICU).
It uses three criteria, assigning one point each:
• Low blood pressure (SBP≤100 mmHg)
• High respiratory rate (≥22 breaths per min)
• Altered mental status
The qSOFA suggests potentially life-threatening sepsis when at least two of the parameters are
met.
4775
Note :
The primary method to evaluate the risk of organ dysfunction (sepsis) in patients with suspected
infection is by determining the sequential (sepsis-related) organ failure assessment (SOFA) score;
which includes an assessment of:
• Circulation - Systolic BP & need for vasopressors; platelet count
• Respiration - PaO2/FiO2 & need for mechanical ventilation
• Mentation - Glasgow Coma Score
• Hepatic function - Bilirubin
• Renal function - Creatinine
Organ dysfunction is identified by a SOFA score of ≥ 2
The SOFA score is limited by its requirement of multiple laboratory tests which makes it complex
and time-consuming. In order to simplify the scoring process, the "Sepsis -3" task force
recommended the quick sepsis-related organ dysfunction assessment "qSOFA" score.

subject: Surgery
question: Which of the following is true regarding inguinal hernia?
explanation:
The medial border of the triangle of doom is bounded by vas deferens and the medial border of
the myopectineal orifice is bounded by the lateral border of the rectus abdominis.
The triangle of doom is bounded
• Medially by the vas deferens
• Laterally by the vessels of the spermatic cord (gonadal vessels)
• Inferiorly by peritoneal reflection
The myopectineal orifice of Fruchaud is an osseo-myo-aponeurotic tunnel through which all groin
hernias occur.
It is bounded:
• Medially by the lateral border of the rectus sheath.
• Laterally by the iliopsoas muscle.
• Above by the arched fibers of the internal oblique and transversus abdominis muscle.
• Below by the pectin pubis and fascia covering it.
4776

[Surgery-AIIMS-2020 (May and Nov INI-CET)-39-exp-image]

subject: Surgery
question:
Match the following:
4743

[Surgery-AIIMS-2020 (May and Nov INI-CET)-40-image]
explanation:
A-3, B-1, C-2, and D-4 is the correct option.
A: Uro bags are used for the collection of urine. They are usually attached to Foley's catheter,
which is placed to drain the bladder (commonly in postoperative patients).
B: Nasogastric tube/Ryle’s tube is used for nasogastric feeding/stomach wash/aspiration.
C: An underwater seal bag is used for the collection of drainage fluid from the thoracic cavity. It
acts as a one-way valve allowing air to leave the pleural cavity, but it cannot be drawn back in by
the negative pressure that is created in the intrathoracic cavity.
D: A metallic suction cannula is used to clear the operative field by suctioning secretions/blood
and aspirating the fluid from various cysts and in a procedure like a hydrocele.

subject: Surgery
question:
A 40-year-old man presents to the ER with severe abdominal pain. On examination, his HR is
110bpm, BP- 88/60mmHg. On per abdomen examination, guarding is present. The erect
abdominal X-ray is shown below. Which of the following would you proceed with, for the
management of this case?

[Surgery-AIIMS-2020 (May and Nov INI-CET)-41-image]
4744
explanation:
The given clinical scenario and erect X-ray abdomen showing gas under the diaphragm are
suggestive of pneumoperitoneum, which is managed by initial resuscitation with IV fluids
followed by exploratory laparotomy.
Pneumoperitoneum is the presence of air or gas in the peritoneal cavity. The most common cause
of pneumoperitoneum is a perforation of a hollow viscus.  Other causes of pneumoperitoneum in
adults include peritoneal dialysis, and mechanical ventilation, after procedures like colonoscopy
and endoscopy. In children, it can be caused by necrotizing enterocolitis, hirschsprung's disease,
meconium ileus and mechanical ventilation.
The patient presents with abdominal pain and distension, vomiting, fever, constipation,
tachycardia, tachypnea, hypotension, and reduced urine output. Per-abdomen examination can
show guarding and rigidity.
4777
An erect X-ray abdomen is highly sensitive for the detection of free intraperitoneal gas. Some of
the signs seen in the X-ray include:
• Cupola sign is the presence of air underneath the diaphragm, giving a dome-like appearance.
• Rigler's sign is where both bowel surfaces are seen together as a sharp margin.
• Falciform ligament sign is the outlining of the falciform ligament with free abdominal gas.
• Liver/gallbladder edge sign is where the sharp margins of the liver/gallbladder are visible due to
the collection of air.
Management includes,
• Adequate preoperative resuscitation with intravenous fluids followed by correction of electrolyte
imbalance, instituting broad-spectrum intravenous antibiotics and
• Early surgical intervention/laparotomy to address the cause of pneumoperitoneum and prevent
further contamination of the peritoneal cavity.
4778

subject: Surgery
question:
Which of the following are indications for surgical intervention in abdominal aortic
aneurysms?

options.a: Size more than 5.5 cm in males
options.b: Size more than 5 cm in females

explanation:
All the given are indications for surgical intervention in abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA). The
indications include:
• Size >5.5 cm in males (option A)
• Size >4.5-5 cm in females (option B)
• Expansion of >0.5 cm within 6 months or >1 cm in one year (option C)
• Painful, tender aneurysm
• Thrombosed aneurysm, aneurysm with distal emboli
The rupture risk is high for aneurysms >5.5 cm and begins to rise exponentially with an
increase in size. Women are at higher risk for rupture than men, with a lower threshold of 4.5 to
5.0 cm (depending on the reference used).
Screening is done for:
• Cardiovascular patients in men and women (60-85 years)
• Men and women >50 years with positive family history
• Annual screening in asymptomatic patients with 4-4.5 cm size, size >4.5 cm once in every 6
months.
Management of AAA can be done by open repair or endovascular aneurysm repair (EVAR). About
75% of infrarenal aneurysms are suitable for endovascular aneurysm repair (EVAR), depending on
the morphology of the aneurysm assessed by a CT scan.
Aneurysms that fall below the mentioned indications may safely be followed with CT or
ultrasound at 6-month intervals since long-term outcomes to routine follow-up are equivalent to
earlier surgical repair of the aneurysm. Smoking cessation is very important and reduces the
mortality rate.
The technique of EVAR:
4788

[Surgery-NEET-2018-1-exp-image]

subject: Surgery
question:
The following image shows the retrograde ureteropyelogram of a patient with loin pain. What
is the most probable clinical diagnosis?

[Surgery-NEET-2018-2-image]
explanation:
The given image showing dilated clubbed calyces with stricture of the left ureteropelvic junction
points to a diagnosis of hydronephrosis.
Hydronephrosis refers to the dilatation of the pelvicalyceal system caused by a partial or complete
blockage of urinary flow.
Investigations:
• Ultrasound is the least invasive technique for diagnosing hydronephrosis.
• Retrograde pyelography is rarely indicated but will confirm the site of obstruction before
corrective surgery.
• Intravenous urogram (IVU) is helpful only if the obstructed kidney is functional. In IVU, the
extrarenal pelvis is dilated and the minor calyces lose their normal cupping. In early stages, there
is blunting of calyces followed by its clubbing in the late stages.
• A renal isotope scan (DTPA or MAG-3) is the best modality to confirm the obstructive dilatation
of the collecting system.

subject: Surgery
question:
4779
Which of the following types of renal stones are produced by laxative abuse?
explanation:
Ammonium urate stones are most likely to be seen in patients with laxative abuse.
Loss of potassium and sodium in laxative abuse causes intracellular acidosis, which increases the
production of ammonia in urine. Dehydration and sodium loss further exacerbate hypokalemia
and reduces the urinary volume, resulting in hyperuricemia. Highly concentrated uric acid and
ammonia promote the formation of ammonium urate stones.
4789
These are also associated with inflammatory bowel disease, chronic diarrhea, ileostomy, recurrent
urinary tract infection and recurrent uric acid stone formation.
The below image shows an ammonium urate stone.

[Surgery-NEET-2018-3-exp-image]

subject: Surgery
question:
A patient is confused, opens his eyes on painful stimuli, flexes one limb on painful stimuli,
and can localize the stimulus with the other limb. His Glasgow Coma Score is ________
explanation:
The GCS in this patient is 11.
• The score for verbal response: 4, because he is confused.
• The score for eye-opening: 2, because his eyes are opening to only painful stimuli.
• The score for best motor response: 5, because he can localize pain.
Therefore, the GCS score is (4+2+5) = 11.

subject: Surgery
question:
A patient presents with a painless swelling of the neck. The histology of the tissue is as given
in the image below. What is the diagnosis?

[Surgery-NEET-2018-5-image]
explanation:
The slide shows the histopathology of Hashimoto thyroiditis, with:
• Follicular destruction
• Reduced colloid with increased interstitial connective tissue
• Small lymphocyte and plasma cell infiltration
• A well-developed germinal centre may be seen
• Hurthle (Askanazy) cells that have abundant eosinophilic granular cytoplasm lining the follicles
4790

[Surgery-NEET-2018-5-exp-image]
Hashimoto's thyroiditis is the most common cause of hypothyroidism in iodine-sufficient areas. It
is also known as struma lymphomatosa or lymphocytic thyroiditis. It is an autoimmune disease
that results in the destruction of the thyroid gland and gradual & progressive thyroid failure.
Clinical features:
• Most prevalent between 45-65 years of age
• More common in females
• Painless enlargement of the thyroid
• Hashitoxicosis: When the auto-antibodies attack and damage the follicles, there is a sudden
release of thyroid hormones from these follicles. This leads to a transient phase of thyrotoxicosis.
• Hypothyroidism: Since the follicles are damaged and cannot form the hormones anymore, these
patients tend to be in a state of hypothyroidism.
Diagnosis:
• T3 and T4 are low
• TSH is raised
• Autoantibodies against:
• Thyroid receptors
• Thyroid peroxidase (TPO)
• Thyroglobulin
These patients are managed with thyroid hormone replacement therapy.
In patients with long-standing goiter, there is a risk of compressive features and the development
of thyroid lymphoma. Such cases can be treated with total thyroidectomy. It may also predispose
to papillary thyroid carcinoma.
Other options:
4791
Option A: Histological features of medullary carcinoma of the thyroid include polygonal to
spindle-shaped cells in the form of nests, trabeculae or follicles. Small, round anaplastic cells may
be found in some cases and amyloid deposits derived from calcitonin polypeptides are present in
the stroma.
The given slide below shows medullary carcinoma of thyroid:

[Surgery-NEET-2018-5-exp-image]
Option C: Grave's disease is an autoimmune disorder characterized by hyperthyroidism due to
circulating autoantibodies. The thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulins bind to the TSH receptors,
causing overgrowth of the thyroid gland and increased thyroid hormone synthesis. Histologically,
there is acinar cell hypertrophy and hyperplasia with the absence of normal colloid in the tall
columnar epithelium (normally the epithelium is flat with colloid). Lymphoid infiltrates,
consisting predominantly of T cells, along with scattered B cells and mature plasma cells, are
present throughout the interstitium. Germinal centers are common.
The given slide below shows Grave's disease:

[Surgery-NEET-2018-5-exp-image]
4792

subject: Surgery
question:
A 45-year-old male patient who presented with a scrotal swelling underwent an orchidectomy.
The image below shows the histopathology findings. What is your diagnosis?

[Surgery-NEET-2018-6-image]
explanation:
Based on the gross and microscopic appearance of the specimen, the diagnosis is seminoma.
The gross specimen shows a well-circumscribed, pale, gray-white, fleshy, homogenous mass, with
a lobulated cut surface, usually devoid of hemorrhage or necrosis. Histopathology shows uniform
tumor cells with abundant clear cytoplasm, distinct cell borders, and large central nuclei. There is
a sparse lymphocytic infiltrate. Classically, they have a fried egg appearance.
Other options:
Option B: Yolk-sac tumor has a yellowish-white, mucinous appearance. Histologically, they are
typically composed of a lace-like (reticular) network of medium-sized cuboidal, flattened, or
spindled cells, which generally show less cytologic atypia than embryonal carcinoma. In
approximately 50% of tumors, structures resembling endodermal sinuses (Schiller-Duval bodies)
may be seen; these consist of a mesodermal core with a central capillary and a visceral and
parietal layer of cells resembling primitive glomeruli.

[Surgery-NEET-2018-6-exp-image]
Option C: Embryonal cell carcinoma appears variegated with foci of hemorrhage or necrosis. 
Histologically, the cells grow in alveolar or tubular patterns, sometimes with papillary folds. More
undifferentiated lesions may display sheets of cells with cleft-like spaces. The neoplastic cells have
an epithelial appearance, are large and anaplastic, and have hyperchromatic nuclei with
prominent nucleoli. The cell borders are usually indistinct, and there is considerable variation in
cell and nuclear size and shape (pleomorphism). Mitotic figures and tumor giant cells are
frequently seen.
4793

[Surgery-NEET-2018-6-exp-image]
Option D: Teratomas also have a variegated appearance but have cysts reflecting the multiplicity
of tissue types. Histologically, collections of differentiated cells or organoid structures originating
in all 3 germ layers can be seen. Elements may be mature (resembling various adult tissues) or
immature (sharing histologic features with fetal or embryonic tissue).

[Surgery-NEET-2018-6-exp-image]

subject: Surgery
question:
A surgeon sees a necrotic bowel when operating on a child for an inguinal hernia. What is
true about the resection and anastomosis of the bowel?

options.a: The surgeon must use chromic catgut
options.b: The surgeon must not include the submucosa in the sutures
options.c: The surgeon must use the single-layer, extramucosal suture technique
options.d: The surgeon must use the single-layer seromuscular suture technique

explanation:
The standard technique used currently for resection anastomosis of the bowel is a one-layer extra
mucosal suture technique since it causes the least tissue necrosis and luminal narrowing.
Interrupted and continuous single-layer suture techniques are adequate and safe.
The extra mucosal suture must also include the submucosa because this layer has a high collagen
content and it is the most stable suture layer in all sections of the gastrointestinal tract.
4794
Catgut has now been replaced with absorbable synthetic polymers. Stapling devices can be used
when speed is required.

subject: Surgery
question:
4781
A lady comes with a reddish firm nodule on her chest which hasn't grown in size. Its
histolopathological image is shown below. What is the diagnosis?

[Surgery-NEET-2018-8-image]
explanation:
The given clinical scenario of a woman with reddish firm nodule on her chest and histology
showing multiple dilated vascular structures with a single layer of epithelial cells is suggestive of a
hemangioma.
Hemangioma is a benign tumor caused by the abnormal growth of blood vessels. Types of
hemangioma are capillary hemangioma (most common), juvenile/ strawberry hemangioma,
cavernous hemangioma and pyogenic hemangioma.
Indications for immediate treatment are complications such as ulceration or those pertaining to
the location of the mass (e.g. visual issues due to a large hemangioma over the eyelid).
Treatment options include:
• Beta-blockers - oral propranolol
• Steroids - intralesional triamcinolone, topical clobetasol, systemic steroids
• Interferon alfa-2a and vincristine - for steroid-resistant lesions
• Local resection with cutting cautery or carbon dioxide laser
The given image shows a flat to elevated, irregular, pinkish-red mass over the skin of the abdomen
suggestive of a strawberry hemangioma.

[Surgery-NEET-2018-8-exp-image]
The given image shows a capillary hemangioma in a child
4795

[Surgery-NEET-2018-8-exp-image]

subject: Surgery
question: Which is the most common organism causing acute bacterial prostatitis?
explanation:
The most common organism causing acute bacterial prostatitis (ABP) is Escherichia coli.
It is commonly caused by an ascending urethral infection or reflux of infected urine into prostatic
ducts. Sexually transmissible infections and other urinary pathogens, such as Staphylococcus
aureus and Streptococcus faecalis may also be responsible.
The patient presents with fever, perineal pain, chills and rigor, and dysuria. Per rectal
examination will reveal a tender, boggy prostate. Care should be taken to avoid aggressive
palpation or prostatic massage as it can lead to bacterial dissemination and sepsis.
Urine examination and culture should be done. Urine examination reveals threads in urine.
Management:
Patients with systemic signs of infection should be admitted for IV antibiotics, hydration, and
monitoring of laboratory parameters.
Patients who have no signs of systemic infection, who can tolerate oral intake, and who have no
urinary retention can be treated with oral antibiotics. Antibiotics are given for 2-6 weeks to ensure
the eradication of the infection as antibiotics don't penetrate the prostate capsule easily.
For outpatient therapy: Ciprofloxacin 500 mg oral every 12 hours or levofloxacin 500 mg OD or
trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole double-strength tablet oral every 12 hours can be used.
For hospitalized patients: Intravenous ciprofloxacin or levofloxacin with or without an
aminoglycoside (gentamicin or tobramycin) or intravenous beta-lactam with or without an
aminoglycoside. Gram-positive infections are treated with cephalosporins (cephalexin, cefazolin)
or dicloxacillin and vancomycin.

subject: Surgery
question:
A 35-year-old man came with complaints of dark-colored stools to the OPD. After undergoing
endoscopy, the patient was diagnosed with an upper GI bleed. Which of the following
structures is used as a demarcation point to differentiate it from a lower GI bleed?
explanation:
4796
The ligament of Treitz is used as the demarcation point to differentiate between upper
gastrointestinal bleeding and lower gastrointestinal bleeding. It is a part of the duodenojejunal
junction.
Upper GI bleeding refers to bleeding proximal to the ligament of Treitz, and lower GI bleeding
refers to bleeding distal to the ligament of Treitz.

[Surgery-NEET-2018-10-exp-image]

subject: Surgery
question: Which is the best graft for a femoropopliteal bypass?
explanation:
The great saphenous vein (GSV) is the best as it lasts longer than polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE),
dacron, and human umbilical vein as a graft in a femoropopliteal bypass.
The GSV is the conduit of choice for arterial bypass grafts below the inguinal ligament.
For bypass above the inguinal ligament, while both GSV and PTFE grafts have similar patency
rates in the first 2 years, the GSV is superior thereafter and hence preferred over PTFE or any
other grafts.
The ipsilateral GSV is the conduit of choice for femoropopliteal bypass. When it is not available,
contralateral GSV should be used. Other veins are preferred when both ipsilateral and
contralateral GSV is not available and they include a small saphenous vein, cephalic vein, and
basilic vein.
Non-autogenous grafts are used for patients who lack a suitable venous conduit. The most
commonly used prosthetic conduit is PTFE. Recent studies have shown that heparin-bonded
PTFE is superior to compared to PTFE without heparin.

subject: Surgery
question: What is the most common cause of chronic pancreatitis?
explanation:
Alcohol consumption is the most common cause of chronic pancreatitis, accounting for 60–70%
of the cases. Smoking increases the risk of alcohol-induced chronic pancreatitis.
4797
Cystic fibrosis is the most common cause of chronic pancreatitis in the pediatric age group.
Other causes of chronic pancreatitis include:
• Pancreatic divisum
• Annular pancreas
• Hyperlipidemia
• Hypercalcemia
• Hyperparathyroidism
• Chronic duct obstruction
• Autoimmune pancreatitis
• Cystic fibrosis
• Tropical pancreatitis
• Hereditary pancreatitis

subject: Surgery
question:
A probe is inserted into the duct from which the nipple discharge is emerging. A tennis
racquet incision is made and the flap is dissected to reach the duct. The duct is then excised.
This procedure is known as?

options.d: Wide excision of the lactiferous duct.

explanation:
Microdochectomy is the excision of the entire lactiferous duct (single duct disease). It is done for
duct papilloma.
Microdochectomy:
• In a duct papilloma where no palpable mass is present, the duct is expressed to observe the
discharge.
• A fine probe or stiff nylon suture is gently inserted into the duct, and the probe is secured to the
nipple skin using silk sutures to prevent displacement during the procedure.
• A tennis racquet shaped incision is made to elevate the skin flaps, and the duct with the probe is
excised along with the skin flap for 5 cm until the papilloma is identified (The papilloma will
always be located within 5 cm from the duct orifice).
• The wound is closed without a drain, and a compression bandage is applied.
It is ideal to rule out DCIS by frozen section biopsy.
Cone excision and wide excision are done under major duct excision (Adair-Haldfield operation).
It is done for duct ectasias with multiple duct involvement and multiple papillomas.

subject: Surgery
question: Which of the following is true about chromic catgut?

options.a:
It is made from cat gut
4783
options.b: It is made from rabbit gut
options.c: It is absorbed by enzymatic degradation and macrophage phagocytosis
options.d: It is tanned with nickel salts to improve handling and to resist degradation in tissue

explanation:
Chromic catgut is absorbed through macrophage phagocytosis and enzymatic degradation within
90 days.
It is made from collagen derived from healthy sheep or cattle. It is then tanned with chromium
salts to improve handling and resist degradation in tissue.
4798
The tensile strength lasts for 21-28 days. It has a moderate tissue reaction as compared to plain
catgut, which has a high tissue reactivity.
It is used to ligate superficial vessels, suture subcutaneous tissues, and is used for stomas and
other tissues that heal rapidly. It is not used in tissues that heal slowly and require prolonged
support.

subject: Surgery
question:
Identify the instrument given in the image below:

[Surgery-NEET-2018-15-image]
explanation:
The given image shows a Morris retractor with a single, flat transversely curved blade and a blunt
projecting ridge with backward projection.
Morris retractor can be single or double-bladed. It is used to retract abdominal wall incisions, loin
incisions, subcostal wounds or any wounds in depth.
It can be used to improve visibility on one side of an incision. Thus, it is valuable during the initial
phase of a laparotomy.

[Surgery-NEET-2018-15-exp-image]
Other options:
Option A: Deaver's retractor is a question mark-shaped stout, atraumatic retractor with a broad,
gently curved long blade. It is used to retract the abdominal wall and viscera. Moist mops should
be kept underneath prior to placing this retractor. It is used for major abdominal surgeries,
bladder surgeries and lumbar sympathectomy.
4799

[Surgery-NEET-2018-15-exp-image]
Option C: Czerny's retractor is a superficial retractor with a thick and small blade on one side and
a biflanged hook on the other side in opposite directions. It is used in hernia surgery and
laparotomy, especially during closure.

[Surgery-NEET-2018-15-exp-image]
Option D: Double-hook retractor consists of two hooks with a handle. It is used in excision of
swellings like lipoma, neurofibroma and cyst. It is also used to hold the skin flaps when raising
them.
4800

[Surgery-NEET-2018-15-exp-image]

subject: Surgery
question: Extra mammary Paget's disease is commonly seen in the:
explanation:
Extramammary Paget's disease arises most frequently on the vulva. Other sites include the
perineum, scrotum, perianal skin, and penis.
Extramammary Paget's disease (EMPD) can be primary or secondary.
• Primary EMPD is more common and occurs in the absence of an underlying malignancy,
thought to arise from intraepidermal parts of apocrine glands or from pluripotent cells from the
epidermis.
• Secondary EMPD arises from an underlying adnexal carcinoma or internal malignancy due to
the epidermotropic spread of malignant cells from the underlying tumor.
Visceral malignancies associated with EMPD are carcinomas of the colon, rectum, bladder,
urethra, cervix, and prostate. The patient presents with well-defined, moist, erythematous scaly
plaques and severe pruritis. Lichenification and excoriations may also be seen.
Diagnosis is confirmed by punch, wedge, or excisional skin biopsy. A systemic workup should also
be done to identify any underlying malignancy and they include colonoscopy, cystoscopy,
colposcopy, and pelvic examination with pap smear in females.
Treatment:
• Mohs microscopic surgery with 5 cm margin clearance
• Radiotherapy
• Topical 5-fluorouracil and imiquimod
• Chemotherapy
• Photodynamic therapy
The given image below shows extramammary Paget's disease of the vulva.
4801

[Surgery-NEET-2018-16-exp-image]

subject: Surgery
question:
4784
The best method to obtain tissue for the diagnosis of pancreatic carcinoma is:

options.a: Transgastric FNAC guided by endoscopic ultrasound

explanation:
Transgastric or transduodenal FNAC or trucut biopsy performed under endoscopic ultrasound
(EUS) guidance is the best method to obtain tissue for the diagnosis of pancreatic carcinoma. This
is because it avoids the spillage of tumor cells into the peritoneal cavity.
Endoscopic ultrasonography with a fine needle aspiration (EUS-FNA) is done if CT has failed to
demonstrate a tumor. When a tissue diagnosis is required prior to surgery to distinguish between
inflammation and neoplasia, EUS-FNA is helpful.
Diagnostic laparoscopy prior to resection can spare an unnecessary laparotomy by identifying
small peritoneal and liver metastases.
Percutaneous transperitoneal biopsy of potentially resectable pancreatic tumors is avoided.

subject: Surgery
question:
An abdominal mass in a patient with congenital hypertrophic pyloric stenosis is best seen
_________.

options.c: During palpation over the left hypochondrium

explanation:
In a child with congenital hypertrophic pyloric stenosis (CHPS), the abdominal mass is best seen
during test feed in the right upper quadrant.
Hypertrophic pyloric stenosis most presents with non-bilious projectile vomiting. It is
common between 2 and 8 weeks of age. Hypertrophy of the circular muscle of the pylorus results
in constriction and obstruction of the gastric outlet. Male infants are at a higher risk.
Clinically, when the infant is given a test feed, an olive-shaped mass is palpable in the right upper
quadrant and visible peristalsis is usually observed moving from left to right across the abdomen.
Infants with hypertrophic pyloric stenosis develop a characteristic metabolic
abnormality- hypochloraemic hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis. Persistent vomiting results in
loss of hydrochloric acid leading to the development of metabolic alkalosis (due to loss of H+)
and hypochloremia (due to loss of Cl-).
4802
All infants with congenital hypertrophic pyloric stenosis are evaluated with an ultrasound
(investigation of choice). It confirms the diagnosis in the majority of cases.
Management: Rehydration and metabolic correction prior to definitive surgery - Ramstedt's
pyloromyotomy.

subject: Surgery
question: While performing a fasciotomy for compartment syndrome, the following layers are incised:

options.c: Skin, Subcutaneous tissue, Superficial fascia and Deep fascia
options.d: Skin, Subcutaneous tissue, Superficial fascia, Deep fascia and a few muscular fibres

explanation:
The incision for a fasciotomy involves the skin, subcutaneous tissue, superficial fascia, and deep
fascia.
Fasciotomy involves incising the deep muscle fascia and is best carried out via longitudinal
incisions of skin, subcutaneous fat, and superficial and deep fascia. The muscle will be seen
bulging out through the fasciotomy opening.
The lower limb can be decompressed via two incisions, each being lateral to the subcutaneous
border of the tibia. This gives access to the two posterior compartments and the leg's peroneal and
anterior compartments.

[Surgery-NEET-2018-19-exp-image]

subject: Surgery
question: Which of the following statements is true about Crohn's disease?

options.a: For effective treatment of a Crohn's mass, only antibiotics are prescribed.
options.b: Symptomatic relapse after surgery is not related to the presence of disease during resection.
options.c: The proximal ileum is most commonly involved in Crohn's disease.
options.d:
On endoscopic examination, continuous lesions are visualised, which are diagnostic of Crohn's
disease.

explanation:
Symptomatic relapse of Crohn's disease after surgery is not related to the presence of the disease
during resection.
The course of the disease after surgery is unpredictable since surgery does not cure Crohn's
disease. Instead, the probability of relapse increases with the time since resection.
Other options:
Option A: Antibiotics alone cannot treat a Crohn's mass effectively. Abscesses can be treated with
metronidazole and ciprofloxacin and should also undergo drainage (and resection if needed).
4803
Option C: The terminal ileum is most commonly involved in Crohn's disease.
Option D: On endoscopic examination, discontinuous "skip" lesions are visualized. These are
irregular areas of inflamed mucosa that are ulcerated with mucopurulent exudate separated by
intervening, normal-appearing intestinal mucosa.

subject: Surgery
question:
4785
Which of the following is the earliest sign in Crohn's disease?
explanation:
Aphthous ulcers, surrounded by a rim of erythematous mucosa, are the earliest lesions seen in a
patient with Crohn's disease during endoscopy.
Crohn's colitis usually spares the rectum and is most severe in the ileocaecal region.
• In early Crohn's disease, small, punched-out aphthous ulcers are often seen (the result of
submucosal lymphoid follicle expansion).
• In moderate disease, the aphthous ulcers coalesce into larger ulcers, which may take on the
appearance of a star (stellate ulcers).
• As disease severity increases, submucosal edema and injury can result in cobblestoning of the
mucosa (option A) and eventually the formation of serpiginous ulcers, which are typical findings
in Crohn's disease.
Thorn-like extraluminal projections in barium studies are known as rose-thorn appearance, and
are a typical sign of Crohn's disease. They represent deep penetrating linear ulcers or fissurings
typically seen within stenosed terminal ileum with a thickened wall. (Options C and D)

subject: Surgery
question: The Van Nuys grading system for ductal carcinoma in situ does not include:
explanation:
Staining for estrogen and progesterone receptors is not part of the Van Nuys system used for
grading DCIS.
The components of the Van Nuys prognostic index (VNPI) are as follows:
• Size of the tumor
• Extent of resection margin
• Histological variant of DCIS - based on nuclear grade, presence of necrosis, presence of
microcalcification
• Age of the patient
Patients with a high score in this grading system benefit from radiotherapy after excision, whereas
those with low-grade and completely excised tumors, need no further treatment.
A score ranging from 1 (lesions with the best prognosis) to 3 (lesions with the worst prognosis) is
given for each of the four prognostic predictors. Total scores range from 4 (least likely to recur) to
12 (most likely to recur).
Note: The type of microcalcification seen on imaging, helps in determining the nuclear grade of
DCIS. While the linear branching type of microcalcification is associated with high nuclear grade,
fine granular type is associated with low nuclear grade.
4804

subject: Surgery
question: Which of the following statements are true with regards to a keloid scar?

options.a: The extent does not cross the wound margins
options.b: Wide excision of the keloid scar prevents recurrence
options.c: Histopathology of a keloid scar shows reduced collagen and increased vascularity
options.d: A keloid scar forms due to increase in the level of growth factors

explanation:
Keloid scars are associated with elevated levels of growth factor leading to increased proliferation
of fibrous tissue.
A keloid scar is defined as excessive scar tissue that extends beyond the boundaries of the original
incision or wound (option A) and rarely regresses on its own.  It is more common in
darker-pigmented ethnicities.
It can arise from surgery, burns, skin inflammation, infections, ear piercing, and even
spontaneously. It occurs 3 months to years after the initial insult with even minor injuries causing
keloid formation.
It most commonly occurs on the skin of the earlobe, deltoid, presternal, upper back, and rarely
extends into the subcutaneous tissue.
4805

[Surgery-NEET-2018-23-exp-image]
The histology of keloid scars shows excess collagen and hypervascularity with collagen fibres
arranged haphazardly in loose sheets (option C).
Treatment: Keloid scars do not improve spontaneously with time and excision alone has a high
rate of recurrence (option B). The following can be done:
• Pressure - local moulds or elastic garments.
• Silicone gel sheeting.
• Intralesional triamcinolone injection.
• Excision and steroid injections.
• Excision and postoperative radiation (external beam or brachytherapy).
• Intralesional excision.
• Laser to reduce redness.
• Vitamin E and palm oil massage.

subject: Surgery
question: Heller’s operation is done for _____.
explanation:
The most common surgical procedure for achalasia cardia is laproscopic Heller myotomy in
conjugation with partial fundoplication.

subject: Surgery
question:
Which of the following is not true about Barrett's esophagus?
4786

options.a: It is the intestinal metaplasia of esophagus
options.b: Mucus secreting foveolar cells are characteristic
options.c: Alician blue is used in the staining of biopsy specimen
options.d: It is a reversible cell adaptation

explanation:
Mucus-secreting goblet cells (not foveolar cells) are characteristic of Barrett's esophagus, as there
is intestinal metaplasia of the esophagus. Foveolar cells are the mucus-secreting cells in the
stomach.
Barrett's esophagus is a complication of chronic gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), and it
is estimated to occur in about 10% of individuals with symptomatic GERD. Symptoms include
heartburn, regurgitation and sometimes dysphagia. Obese white men over the age of 60
years represent the highest-risk group.
Intestinal metaplasia in the esophagus occurs due to chronic irritation of the esophagus in GERD
(option A) since intestinal epithelium is better suited to handling gastric acid exposure. Hence,
mucus-secreting goblet cells are characteristic of Barrett's esophagus. Since all metaplasia are
reversible, Barrett's esophagus is also reversible (option D). Only 0.2 to 1% of Barrett's esophagus
leads to dysplasia and adenocarcinoma.
4807

[Surgery-NEET-2018-25-exp-image]
Alician blue is used in the staining of the biopsy specimen, as it stains the goblet cells (option C).
Mucicarmine is the specific stain.
Seattle protocol is used for the management of Barrett's esophagus.

[Surgery-NEET-2018-25-exp-image]

subject: Surgery
question: Modified shock index is calculated using?
explanation:
• Modified shock index = HR/MAP
• Original shock index = HR/SBP
The shock index is a hemodynamic stability indicator. In patients with shock, it is a
better predictor of mortality than blood pressure or heart rate alone. A shock index >0.9 is
indicative of higher mortality.
4808
The modified shock index is a more sensitive indicator of tissue perfusion than the original shock
index. In critically ill patients, the DBP decreases earlier than SBP, hence, the modified shock
index using MAP is better suited to assess the severity of shock.
4809

subject: Surgery
question:
The type of knot seen in the image is:

[Surgery-NEET-2019-1-image]
explanation:
The given image shows the suture material exiting the knot is on the same side as the entry i.e no
cross-over is seen. This is seen in a reef knot.
A knot is a fastening made of threads intertwined together or on themselves. Each knot consists
of- 2 half-hitches placed in sequential throws. Squaring refers to tightening the knot/half-hitch in a
manner that the threads lay flat due to the alternating direction of half-hitches in adjacent layers.
4817
Types of knots:
• Granny knot: half-hitch over a half-hitch in the same direction. It has better-holding power than
half-hitch
• Reef knot: half-hitch followed by a second half-hitch after squaring.
• Triple throw knot: Reef knot with another half-hitch knot for added security. Knots made with
prolene sutures require 6-8 throws to prevent slippage.
• Surgeon’s knot: Modification of the reef knot with two half-hitches in the first layer.
• Slip knot: Modification of either granny/reef knot formed when one end of the thread is kept taut
while tightening the knot.

[Surgery-NEET-2019-1-exp-image]

subject: Surgery
question: Which of the following drugs causes carcinoma bladder?
explanation:
Cyclophosphamide (an alkylating agent) causes bladder cancer
The risk of bladder cancer with cyclophosphamide is directly proportional to the duration and
intensity of cyclophosphamide treatment. Phosphoramide mustard is the primary mutagenic
metabolite that causes bladder cancer in patients exposed to cyclophosphamide.
Painless, gross hematuria is the most common symptom that is indicative of a bladder carcinoma
until proven otherwise.
Depending upon the depth of invasion, bladder carcinoma can be divided into
• Flat, non-invasive carcinoma in situ (CIS)
• Non-muscle invasive bladder cancer (70% of cases) - does not invade detrusor muscle but can
invade lamina propria.
• Muscle-invasive bladder cancer - invades the detrusor muscle.
Diagnosis is by cystourethroscopy (directly visualizing the growth) and biopsy. Management is as
follows:
4818
• Non-muscle invasive bladder cancer:
• Transurethral resection of bladder tumor (TURBT) followed by intravesical chemotherapy with
mitomycin C or epirubicin
• Immunotherapy with regular intravesical instillations of Bacille Calmette Guerin (BCG) in
high-risk tumors.
• Muscle invasive bladder cancer: Radical cystectomy along with urinary diversion procedure and
lymph node dissection +/- neoadjuvant chemotherapy
Other options:
Option B: Cisplatin is an anticancer drug used to treat testicular cancer. It is associated with an
increased risk for acute myeloid leukemia (AML).
Option C: Taxane is an anticancer drug and is the main component in the treatment regimens of
metastatic cancers. Taxane's adverse effects are neutropenia and fluid retention (pulmonary
edema).
Option D: Tamoxifen is a selective estrogen receptor modulator (SERM) and is used in the
treatment of estrogen-dependent breast cancer. Due to its partial agonistic activity, it is known to
increase endometrial cancer in post-menopausal women.

subject: Surgery
question:
The most common site of branchial cyst is ______.
4810

options.a: Junction of upper and middle third of the sternomastoid muscle
options.b: Junction of middle and lower third of the sternomastoid muscle
options.d: Between the two heads of the sternomastoid muscle

explanation:
The most common site of a branchial cyst is at the junction of the upper and middle third of the
sternomastoid muscle at its anterior border.
The below image shows a large right-sided branchial cyst.

[Surgery-NEET-2019-3-exp-image]
Branchial cyst develops due to the persistence of the cervical sinus. The cervical sinus is formed
when the 2nd arch mesoderm overgrows the succeeding arches and overhangs them as shown in
the image. The space between the overhanging 2nd pharyngeal arch and the 3rd, 4th, and 6th
arches is called the cervical sinus. The cavity of the cervical sinus is lined by ectoderm and
4819
normally gets obliterated.
Branchial cysts are usually lined by squamous epithelium and contain thick, turbid fluid full of
cholesterol crystals. It is fluctuant and may transilluminate when it contains clear fluid.
A branchial fistula may be acquired or congenital. The congenital fistula is seen at the junction of
the middle and lower third of the sternomastoid (option B). An acquired fistula is usually seen in
cases of incomplete surgery for the branchial cyst or spontaneous rupture, and it is seen at the
same site as the cyst.

[Surgery-NEET-2019-3-exp-image]

subject: Surgery
question: Dohlman’s procedure is for:
explanation:
Dohlman’s procedure is done for Zenker’s diverticulum.
It is a transoral endoscopic approach, that ablates the septum between the diverticulum and the
cervical esophagus. It can be used for diverticula between 2 and 5 cm in size.
Other options:
Option A: Meckel’s diverticulum is a remnant of the vitellointestinal duct and is treated by
surgical resection.
Option C: Bochadlek’s hernia is a congenital posterolateral defect on the left side of the diaphragm
due to defective embryological development.
Option D: Menetrier’s disease is a premalignant condition with gross hypertrophy of gastric
mucosal folds and is treated with gastrectomy.

subject: Surgery
question: Parathyroid gland post surgery is commonly transferred to:
explanation:
The parathyroid is commonly transferred to the brachioradialis muscle post-surgery. This will
facilitate easy removal if there is disease recurrence after surgery.
4820
Parathyroidectomy is indicated in:
• Symptomatic primary hyperparathyroidism (HPT):
• Asymptomatic primary HPT with:
• raise in serum calcium >1mg/dl of the normal range
• >400 mg urinary calcium in 24-hour urine test
• < -2.5 Z-score of bone mineral density
• Age <50yr and failed medical treatment
• Severe cases of secondary HPT
Parathyroidectomy is performed in neck hyperextension (semi-Fowler position). The thyroid
gland is mobilized, and the parathyroid gland with adenoma is identified and resected. The
parathyroid tissue is transplanted to the non-dominant forearm.
Pockets are made in the belly of the brachioradialis muscle, and one to two pieces of parathyroid
tissue measuring 1 mm each are placed into each pocket. A total of 12 to 14 pieces are
transplanted. This is a type of heterotopic transplant.
Miami criteria (confirm the absence of another hyperactive parathyroid): A drop of parathyroid
hormone intraoperatively, to more than half the maximum value at 10 minutes postresection is
considered an adequate resection in a parathyroid adenoma. However, if there is no drop in PTH
level, then a multi-gland disease is suspected and the surgery is converted to a bilateral neck
exploration.
Complications of parathyroidectomy include:
• Persistent or recurrent hyperparathyroidism
• Hypoparathyroidism (presenting as hypocalcemia)
• Hungry bone syndrome
• Injury to the recurrent laryngeal nerve

subject: Surgery
question: What is the drug used for estrogen dependant breast cancer?
explanation:
Tamoxifen is a selective estrogen receptor modulator (SERM) that is mainly used for estrogen
receptor-positive breast cancers in pre-menopausal women.
It exhibits selective agonistic and antagonistic activity for ER receptors (ERα and ERβ):
• Agonist activity at the uterus, bone, liver, and pituitary
• Antagonist activity in breast and blood vessels
Uses:
• Part of the primary treatment regimen for estrogen receptor-positive (ER+) breast cancers in
pre-menopausal women
• Adjuvant therapy after breast conservative surgery to prevent a recurrence
4821
• Tamoxifen also is effective (a 40%–50% reduction in tumor incidence) in preventing ER+ breast
cancer in women at increased risk.
Adverse effects:
• Vasomotor symptoms (hot flashes) and sweating
• Endometrial cancer
• Increased risk of thromboembolic events
• Fatty liver disease

subject: Surgery
question: The commonest surgical approach to a retrosternal goiter is via:
explanation:
The commonest surgical approach to a retrosternal goiter is via the neck and the most common
type of goitre approached is the secondary retrosternal goiter.
A goiter is considered to be retrosternal if at least 50% of the thyroid tissue is located
intrathoracically. They can be primary or secondary.
Retrosternal goiter presents with dyspnoea, stridor, and dilated neck veins. Pemberton’s sign
(venous congestion of the neck on the raising of hands above the head) is seen.
Contrast-enhanced computed tomography (CECT) of the neck is performed to assess the location
of the thyroid. The definitive treatment is surgical excision of the ectopic thyroid.
Other options:
Option B & C: Median sternotomy (via chest) is indicated in:
• Primary mediastinal goiter
• Malignant retrosternal goiter
• Very large retrosternal goiter
• Recurrence in the mediastinum

subject: Surgery
question:
An intern placed an intercostal drainage tube in a patient. Which of the following steps is
incorrect?

options.a: Identified 5th intercostal space anterior to mid axillary line to place the tube
options.b: Inserted tube along the lower border of upper rib
options.c: Directed tube posteriorly to prevent injury

explanation:
4822
An intercostal tube (ICD) is placed along the upper border of the lower rib at the 5th intercostal
space (ICS) to prevent damage to the neurovascular bundle. Hence, option B is incorrect.
Chest tube insertion (thoracostomy) is indicated in pneumothorax, hemothorax, chylothorax, and
pleural effusion in order to drain air/fluid from the pleural space.
The triangle of safety, usually in the 5th intercostal space, is the safest site for chest tube insertion,
it is bound by:
• anterior to the midaxillary line
• above the horizontal line marked by the level of the nipple
• below and lateral to the pectoralis major muscle

[Surgery-NEET-2019-8-exp-image]

[Surgery-NEET-2019-8-exp-image]
Contraindications include pulmonary adhesions, pulmonary trauma, coagulopathy, and
diaphragmatic hernia.
Complications of this procedure:
4823
• Infection at the site of insertion
• Unintentional injury to the lung - hemothorax
• Intercostal nerve/ vessel injury
• Subcutaneous hematoma/ emphysema

subject: Surgery
question:
What is the size of the given blade used for incision and drainage of a superficial abscess?

[Surgery-NEET-2019-9-image]
explanation:
The size of the surgical blade shown is the 11-size blade (stab knife) that has a triangular shape
with an oblique edge and a sharp-pointed tip.
Uses:
• Incision and drainage (I & D) of an abscess
• Insertion of drains and arteriotomy.
Other options:
Option A: The 10-size blade is used for skin incisions. It has a curved cutting edge that tapers to a
pointed tip.
Option C: The 22-size blade is often used for abdominal incisions. It has a flat, unsharpened back
edge and a curved front cutting edge.
Option D: The 15-size blade is used for minor surgical procedures (lipoma excision) and precise
skin incisions. It is a smaller blade with a small cutting edge.

[Surgery-NEET-2019-9-exp-image]

subject: Surgery
question: In a blast injury, which of the following organs is least vulnerable to the blast wave?
explanation:
In a blast injury, the liver is the least vulnerable to blast wave (primary blast) injury.
4824
Blast injury is a complex type of physical trauma resulting from direct or indirect exposure to an
explosion. Primary blast injuries result from the rapid overpressure or shock waves produced by
an explosion. These injuries result from the dramatic changes in barometric pressure projecting
outwards from the point of detonation. They predominantly cause damage to air-filled hollow
organs of the body from rapid pressure change (barotrauma). This includes the middle ear, lungs,
and GIT.
The most sensitive and most frequently injured hollow organs are the tympanic membranes >
lungs. Blast damage to the lungs is the most common cause of life-threatening injury following an
explosion.
Solid organs are the most resistant to blast injuries as the parenchymal disruption requires very
high levels of overpressure.

subject: Surgery
question:
A man presents with a slowly progressive swelling over the past 1 and 1/2 years. On
examination, it has a variable consistency and appears as shown in the image. What is the
probable cause?

[Surgery-NEET-2019-11-image]
explanation:
A slowly progressive swelling, with a variable consistency, lifting the ear lobe, is suggestive of
pleomorphic adenoma of the parotid gland.
Pleomorphic adenoma, the most common benign salivary gland tumor, primarily affects the
parotid gland, particularly its superficial lobe. It is also referred to as a mixed tumor due to its
combination of epithelial and mesenchymal components. This tumor most commonly occurs in
females around the age of 40.
Clinical features:
• Painless, slow-growing swelling
• Lobulated, firm, variable in consistency
• Lifts the earlobe
• No facial nerve involvement
4825
• Curtain sign positive - cannot be moved above the zygomatic bone.
• In case of deep lobe involvement, tonsils, and faucial pillars will be pushed medially.
• Features of malignancy - a sudden increase in size, ulceration of the overlying skin, facial nerve
palsy (involvement of facial nerve), painful swelling, and restriction of jaw movements.
Fine needle aspiration cytology (FNAC) is the investigation of choice in diagnosing pleomorphic
adenoma. Open biopsy is contraindicated due to the chances of injury to the facial nerve.
All tumors of the superficial lobe of the parotid gland should be managed by superficial
parotidectomy. Radical parotidectomy is performed for malignant pleomorphic adenoma.
Other options:
Option A: Adamantinoma is a low-grade malignant tumor that usually presents with pre-tibial
pain and swelling since it most commonly affects the tibial diaphysis.
Option B:  Dermoid cysts present as firm painless subcutaneous nodules and are of 4 types:
• Sequestration dermoid - sequestration of epithelium during embryological development.
• Tubulodermoid - accumulation of secretions from ectodermal tube/duct
• Implantation dermoid - implantation of the epidermis due to trauma
• Teratomatous dermoid - having all 3 germ layers (ecto, meso, and endoderm)
Option D: Sebaceous cysts are retention cysts of the sebaceous glands that present as smooth,
non-tender, non-transillumination swelling with a punctum (in 70% of the patients).

subject: Surgery
question:
What is shown in the given image?
4813

[Surgery-NEET-2019-12-image]
explanation:
The image shows a dark-colored summit over a swelling, usually seen in a sebaceous cyst.
Sebaceous cysts are the retention cysts of the sebaceous gland that are usually present on the face,
scalp, or scrotum. They contain yellowish-white cheesy material with fat and epithelium.
Clinical features:
• The swelling is painless, soft, and freely mobile.
• Though non-transilluminating, they are Paget's test positive (fluctuant).
• Punctum is seen in 70% of the patients with sebaceous cysts. They are dark in color due to the
accumulation of denuded epithelium.
• They also have an unpleasant sebum odor.
• The swelling can ulcerate and become painful (Cock’s peculiar tumor).
• Some cysts over the scalp may also harden over time (Sebaceous horn).
• Many may get infected and lead to abscess formation.
Treatment:
• Excision including skin adjacent to punctum using elliptical incision-dissection method.
• Incision and avulsion of cyst wall.
4826
• In the presence of an abscess, an initial treatment with an incision later followed by excision of
the cyst
Other options:
Option A: Dermoid cysts present as firm painless subcutaneous nodules and are of 4 types:
• Sequestration dermoid - sequestration of epithelium during embryological development
• Tubulodermoid - accumulation of secretions from ectodermal tube/duct
• Implantation dermoid - implantation of the epidermis due to trauma
• Teratomatous dermoid - having all 3 germ layers (ecto, meso, and endoderm)
Option C: Meningiomas are benign tumors arising from the meningothelial cells of the dura. They
present with headaches, seizures, or focal neurological deficits.
Option D: Postauricular cutaneous mastoid fistula is a rare complication of chronic otitis media
that heals very slowly and needs surgical closure.

subject: Surgery
question:
A female patient presents with a breast abscess. An aspiration was attempted under antibiotic
cover which failed to reveal any aspirate. What is the next step in management?

options.d: Stop antibiotics as no aspirate was collected

explanation:
In a patient with a breast abscess, the next step after a failed aspiration is to attempt aspiration
once again under antibiotic cover.
Incision and drainage is not preferred, as it can potentially interfere with breastfeeding, and
delicate structures such as ducts, lobules, and tubules may be injured, possibly leading to a
non-healing milk fistula.
A breast abscess is a complication of acute mastitis, which is more common in new mothers at the
beginning of lactation (lactational mastitis). Non-lactational mastitis is more common in diabetic,
immunosuppressed people and people predisposed by duct ectasia and periareolar infections.
Clinical features of a breast abscess include:
• Fever and malaise
• Localized pain and tenderness
• Redness and swelling of the affected breast
• Positive fluctuation test.
4827

[Surgery-NEET-2019-13-exp-image]
An ultrasonogram of the affected breast is performed to confirm the abscess, and treatment
proceeds as follows:
• Analgesics, antipyretics, and breast support for pain and fever relief.
• Aspiration is performed under antibiotic cover, and the pus is cultured. If aspiration proves
unsuccessful, it should be reattempted after a few days.
• Incision and drainage should be considered in cases of:
• Mastitis unresponsive to antibiotics for 48 hours.
• Brawny induration.
• Progressive mastitis with persistent fever

pportAspiration of Pus under

pirations if requiredIncision 
& drainage

subject: Surgery
question: Which nerve is involved in Frey’s syndrome?
explanation:
The auriculotemporal nerve (V3 nerve) is involved in Frey's syndrome (gustatory sweating).
Frey’s syndrome is also known as Dupuy syndrome or auriculotemporal syndrome. It commonly
develops after parotid surgery. It is caused by an aberrant regeneration of post-ganglionic
parasympathetic nerve fibers crossed along the sympathetic pathways to the sweat glands near the
parotid region.
Clinical features are due to the result of salivary stimulation (by the smell or taste of food) leading
to the sweating and flushing of the periauricular region on the involved side of the face. A starch
iodine test is used for diagnosis.
Treatment ranges from antiperspirants (in mild cases) to tympanic neurectomy or botulinum
toxin injection (in severe cases). It can be prevented by incorporating careful extracapsular
dissection and closure of the superficial musculoaponeurotic layer during parotidectomy.

[Surgery-NEET-2019-14-exp-image]
Other options:
Option A: Facial nerve is the 7th cranial nerve that supplies the muscles of the face, and results in
facial paralysis if injured during parotidectomy
Option B: Oculomotor is the 3rd cranial nerve which supplies the extraocular muscles. If injured
during ophthalmic surgeries, it results in ocular paralysis and the eye pointing down and out.
Option D: Glossopharyngeal nerve is the 9th cranial nerve and its injury causes loss of taste
sensation from the posterior 1/3rd of the tongue.

subject: Surgery
question: Which of the following statements is false regarding deep vein thrombosis (DVT)?

options.c: Most common clinical presentation is pain and tenderness in calf
options.d: Some cases may directly present as pulmonary thromboembolism

explanation:
Most cases of deep vein thrombosis (DVT) are unilateral. It is bilateral in only 30% of the
individuals.
DVT refers to thrombosis of the deep venous system.
4829
Caused by:
• Postoperative thrombosis (most common cause)
• Oral contraceptive pills (OCPs)
• After childbirth
• Immobility
Sites:
• Pelvic veins—common
• Leg veins—common in femoral and popliteal veins (common on the left side).
Fever is the earliest symptom, which is also most commonly accompanied by pain and tenderness
in the calf and thigh (option C).
The following signs could be elicited:
• Positive Homan's sign: Resistance of calf muscles on passive foreceful dorsiflexion of the foot.
• Mose's sign: Gentle squeezing of the lower part of the calf from side to side is painful.
• Neuhof's sign: Thickening and deep tenderness seen on deep palpation of the calf muscles.
Sometimes, the thrombus may get dislodged and present as pulmonary thromboembolism (option
D) with features of dyspnoea, tachypnoea, and chest pain.
Clinical features are unreliable for making a definitive diagnosis (option A), and a venous Doppler
is the best modality for confirming DVT. It reveals a non-compressible dilated vein. The likelihood
of pulmonary embolism can be predicted using modified Wells criteria.
Patients with DVT should be immediately started with subcutaneous low molecular weight
heparin (LMWH). If contraindicated due to renal insufficiency or thrombocytopenia-related
conditions, they should be started on fondaparinux (indirect factor Xa inhibitor) or bivalirudin
(direct thrombin inhibitor). Anticoagulants should be administered for a minimum period of 3
months. Vena cava filters are used in patients with bleeding risks (like hemophilia).

subject: Surgery
question:
A truck driver presents with dilated, tortuous veins of 4 mm in the foot. What is the stage of
clinical classification according to CEAP(Clinical-etiology-anatomy-pathophysiology for
chronic venous disorders)?
explanation: Dilated tortuous veins of 4 mm indicate varicose veins, so the stage is C2.

subject: Surgery
question: Which of the following statements is false regarding rectal carcinoma?

options.a: Hartmann’s operation is done in elderly debilitated patients
options.b: Adenocarcinoma is a rare variant of rectal carcinoma
options.c: Early morning spurious diarrhoea and tenesmus can occur
options.d: Growth confined to the rectal mucosa is stage A of modified Duke’s staging

explanation:
Adenocarcinoma is the most common variant of rectal carcinoma.
Colorectal cancer is:
• The second most common malignancy globally
• The second most common cancer in women
• The third most common cancer in men
4830
• The fourth most common cause of death due to cancer.
Symptoms of rectal carcinoma include:
• Painless bleeding - earliest and most common
• Alteration of bowel habits - change in frequency and consistency of the stool
• Tenesmus - unable to pass a motion even when there is the sensation of needing to evacuate the
bowel. It is suggestive of a tumor of the lower half of the rectum
• Early morning bloody diarrhea (option C) - passage of blood and mucus in addition to feces. Pain
and weight loss are usually late symptoms
Abdominal examination and digital rectal examination are used for the primary evaluation. Rigid
sigmoidoscopy is performed to obtain a biopsy. A synchronous tumor (primary tumor with a
different origin than the one currently investigated) is excluded by performing a colonoscopy.
Modified Duke’s classification:
A
Growth limited up to the rect
al mucosa (option D)
B
B1: Tumor invading muscular
is propria, no lymph node in
vasion.B2: Tumors invading i
nto or through serosa, no ly
mph node invasion.
C
C1: The tumour did not excee
d the bowel wall, lymph node
 metastasis.C2: Tumour exce
eded the intestinal wall and l
ymph node metastasis.
D
Distant metastasis

[Surgery-NEET-2019-17-exp-image]
4831
Surgery is the mainstay of management for rectal carcinoma. Non-operative management is
offered to only those patients who have a complete clinical response to neoadjuvant.
The type of surgery depends on multiple factors (size, location, age of the patient, etc). However,
the general consensus is:
• Early cancer (T1 and a few T2) is locally excised via the transanal approach.
• Most rectal cancer is treated by anterior resection and colorectal/coloanal anastomosis.
• Low extensive tumors are treated by laparoscopic abdominoperineal resection(APR) and
permanent colostomy.
• Hartmann’s operation (option A) is preferred in old and frail patients due to poor anal sphincter
and postoperative incontinence.

subject: Surgery
question: A gasless abdomen is seen in:
explanation:
A gasless abdomen is an X-ray feature that can be seen in acute pancreatitis. Patients with acute
pancreatitis can have significant vomiting and diarrhea that may produce a gasless abdomen.
Common causes of gasless abdomen include:
• High obstruction: gastric outlet obstruction, congenital atresia
• Small bowel obstruction
• Bowel ischemia
• Ascites
• Acute pancreatitis
• Gastroenteritis
• Large abdominal mass - due to displacement
• Post-surgery following total colectomy, esophagogastrectomy, gastrectomy, and low anterior
resection
• A paucity of small bowel gas may be a normal finding in some adults
Note: In intussusception, an X-ray shows small bowel obstruction, a mass, and a paucity of gas in
the right iliac fossa.

subject: Surgery
question: Gall stone chemistry most commonly seen amongst the following is:
explanation:
The most common gallstone chemistry seen is crystalline cholesterol monohydrate.
Types of gallstones:
• Cholesterol stones (75% of gallstones) have >70% cholesterol by weight and are composed of
crystalline cholesterol monohydrate. The majority of cholesterol stones have a mixed composition
with bile pigments and calcium, in addition to the cholesterol. They are the most common type of
gall stones worldwide and in India.
4832
• Pigment stones have <20% cholesterol and are dark due to the presence of calcium
bilirubinate as the major constituent.
• Black pigment stones (20% of gallstones) - are formed due to supersaturation of unconjugated
bilirubin (seen in hereditary hemolytic anemia (like sickle cell and hereditary spherocytosis)
• Brown pigment stones (5% of gallstones) - formed secondary to infections (like E. coli, and
Ascaris lumbricoides)
Note: Pure cholesterol stones are uncommon and account for <10% of all stones.

subject: Surgery
question:
A 56-year-old patient suffering from alcoholic cirrhosis receives a healthy liver from his
biological son. What is this type of transplant known as?
explanation:
Transplant of an organ/tissue from a son to a parent is an example of an allograft.
An allograft is transplant of cells/tissues/organs from a genetically non-identical donor of the same
species.
Isografts are allografts in which cells/tissues/organs are transplanted from a donor to a genetically
identical recipient. (monozygotic twins)

subject: Surgery
question:
Esophageal endoscopy of a patient reveals the presence of serpiginous ulcers. This finding
indicates:
explanation:
The image showing serpiginous ulcers in the otherwise normal mucosa of the distal esophagus is
suggestive of cytomegaloviral esophagitis.

[Surgery-NEET-2019-21-exp-image]

subject: Surgery
question: Which of the following is not a part of revised trauma score (RTS)?
explanation:
Pulse rate is NOT a part of revised trauma score.
4833
The Revised Trauma Score is made up of 3 categories: Glasgow Coma Scale, systolic blood
pressure, and respiratory rate.
The score range is 0–12.
A patient with an RTS score of 12 is labelled delayed, 11 is urgent, and 3–10 is immediate. Those
who have an RTS below 3 are declared dead.
4834

subject: Surgery
question:
Which of the following is true about the flap given in the image.

[Surgery-NEET-2020-1-image]

options.a: Pedicled TRAM based on superior epigastric artery
options.b: Free TRAM based on superior and inferior epigastric artery
options.c: DIEP based on deep inferior epigastric artery
options.d: Lower Abdominal Fat based inferior epigastric vessels

explanation:
The given image showing only skin and fat moved to the chest is suggestive of a deep inferior
epigastric artery perforator flap (DIEP), based on the deep inferior epigastric artery.
4844
DIEP is a variant of TRAM flap and free flap where only the skin and fat are moved to the chest.
A microvascular technique is used in which no muscle is taken and the perforating vessels are
dissected out.
As the muscle is spared in DIEP, there is a reduced risk of abdominal wall weakness and its
associated complications.
Other options:
Options A and B: The pedicled TRAM flap is based on the superior epigastric artery and the free
TRAM flap is based on superior and inferior epigastric arteries (supercharged TRAM flap);
however, the given image depicts a deep inferior epigastric artery flap. In TRAM flaps:
• The skin, fat, and muscle are moved to the chest.
• It provides good cosmesis and bulk since it has a soft tissue consistency similar to the breast.
• Disadvantages include epigastric bulge, partial flap loss, fat necrosis, abdominal wall weakness,
and hernias.
The given image below shows a TRAM flap:

[Surgery-NEET-2020-1-exp-image]

subject: Surgery
question: What is the most common site of gastrinoma in MEN 1 syndrome?
explanation:
The most common site of gastrinoma in MEN 1 syndrome is the duodenum.
Gastrinoma is the most common pancreatic islet tumor in MEN 1 syndrome, arising from the G
cells of the pancreas. Gastrinoma, associated with marked gastric acid production and recurrent
peptic ulcers, is called Zollinger-Ellison syndrome. The majority of gastrinomas occur in the
gastrinoma (Passaro's) triangle.
In MEN 1, they often occur in patients <30 years. Patients present with abdominal pain (the
most common symptom), diarrhea and steatorrhea.
Management:
4845
• Medical management for tumors <2 cm with proton-pump inhibitors.
• Surgery for tumors >2 cm.
• Proton-pump inhibitors with somatostatin analogues for advanced locoregional disease or
metastasis.

subject: Surgery
question:
4835
A 50-year-old male who is a chronic smoker presented to the hospital with intermittent
claudication pain of both thigh and buttock region on walking about 500 m. Which of the
following is a likely diagnosis?

options.a: Arterial disease involving the superficial femoral artery
options.d: Arterial disease involving the profunda femoris artery

explanation:
The given clinical scenario is suggestive of arterial disease with aortoiliac involvement. Classically,
the muscle group affected by claudication is one anatomical level below the level of arterial
disease.
Intermittent claudication is a feature of chronic arterial disease or occlusion of the artery. 
Aortoiliac disease affects the infrarenal aorta and iliac arteries.
Management involves smoking cessation, lifestyle modifications, and treatment of underlying
diabetes or hyperlipidemia if present.
For claudication, cilostazol or pentoxyfilline is used. Cilostazol is superior to pentoxyfilline.
Surgical options include aortoiliac bypass, aortobifemoral bypass, percutaneous transluminal
angioplasty (PTA), which can be with or without stent placement, and thromboendarterectomy.
